title: Exercises: Single Qubit Gates
description: Hands-on exercises to master single qubit gate operations

exercises.title: Implement Basic Gates
exercises.description: Implement the Pauli X, Y, Z gates and verify they are unitary.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

# TODO: Define Pauli gates as numpy arrays
X = # 2x2 matrix
Y = # 2x2 matrix  
Z = # 2x2 matrix

def is_unitary(U):
    """Check if matrix U is unitary: U†U = I"""
    # TODO: Implement unitarity check
    pass

# Test
print('X is unitary:', is_unitary(X))
print('Y is unitary:', is_unitary(Y))
print('Z is unitary:', is_unitary(Z))

# Verify X² = Y² = Z² = I
print('X² = I:', np.allclose(X @ X, np.eye(2)))
print('Y² = I:', np.allclose(Y @ Y, np.eye(2)))
print('Z² = I:', np.allclose(Z @ Z, np.eye(2)))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]])
Y = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]])
Z = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]])

def is_unitary(U):
    return np.allclose(U @ U.conj().T, np.eye(2))

print('X is unitary:', is_unitary(X))
print('Y is unitary:', is_unitary(Y))
print('Z is unitary:', is_unitary(Z))

print('X² = I:', np.allclose(X @ X, np.eye(2)))
print('Y² = I:', np.allclose(Y @ Y, np.eye(2)))
print('Z² = I:', np.allclose(Z @ Z, np.eye(2)))
exercises.expectedOutput: All gates unitary, all squares equal identity

exercises.title: Hadamard Gate Actions
exercises.description: Apply the Hadamard gate to various states and verify the results.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

H = np.array([[1, 1], [1, -1]]) / np.sqrt(2)

# Basis states
ket_0 = np.array([1, 0])
ket_1 = np.array([0, 1])

# TODO: Calculate H|0⟩ and H|1⟩
H_ket_0 = # Your code
H_ket_1 = # Your code

print('H|0⟩ =', H_ket_0)
print('H|1⟩ =', H_ket_1)

# TODO: Verify H² = I by applying H twice to |0⟩
H_H_ket_0 = # Your code
print('H(H|0⟩) =', H_H_ket_0)
print('Back to |0⟩:', np.allclose(H_H_ket_0, ket_0))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

H = np.array([[1, 1], [1, -1]]) / np.sqrt(2)

ket_0 = np.array([1, 0])
ket_1 = np.array([0, 1])

H_ket_0 = H @ ket_0
H_ket_1 = H @ ket_1

print('H|0⟩ =', np.round(H_ket_0, 4), '= |+⟩')
print('H|1⟩ =', np.round(H_ket_1, 4), '= |-⟩')

H_H_ket_0 = H @ H @ ket_0
print('H(H|0⟩) =', np.round(H_H_ket_0, 4))
print('Back to |0⟩:', np.allclose(H_H_ket_0, ket_0))

exercises.title: Rotation Gates
exercises.description: Implement rotation gates Rx, Ry, Rz and show that Pauli gates are π rotations.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def Rx(theta):
    """Rotation around X axis by angle theta"""
    c = np.cos(theta / 2)
    s = np.sin(theta / 2)
    # TODO: Return the Rx matrix
    return

def Ry(theta):
    """Rotation around Y axis by angle theta"""
    c = np.cos(theta / 2)
    s = np.sin(theta / 2)
    # TODO: Return the Ry matrix
    return

def Rz(theta):
    """Rotation around Z axis by angle theta"""
    # TODO: Return the Rz matrix
    return

# Verify that Rx(π), Ry(π), Rz(π) give Pauli gates (up to global phase)
X = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]])
Y = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]])
Z = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]])

print('Rx(π) ~ X:', np.allclose(np.abs(Rx(np.pi)), np.abs(X)))
print('Ry(π) ~ Y:', np.allclose(np.abs(Ry(np.pi)), np.abs(Y)))
print('Rz(π) ~ Z:', np.allclose(np.abs(Rz(np.pi)), np.abs(Z)))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def Rx(theta):
    c = np.cos(theta / 2)
    s = np.sin(theta / 2)
    return np.array([[c, -1j*s], [-1j*s, c]])

def Ry(theta):
    c = np.cos(theta / 2)
    s = np.sin(theta / 2)
    return np.array([[c, -s], [s, c]])

def Rz(theta):
    return np.array([[np.exp(-1j*theta/2), 0], [0, np.exp(1j*theta/2)]])

X = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]])
Y = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]])
Z = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]])

print('Rx(π) ~ X:', np.allclose(np.abs(Rx(np.pi)), np.abs(X)))
print('Ry(π) ~ Y:', np.allclose(np.abs(Ry(np.pi)), np.abs(Y)))
print('Rz(π) ~ Z:', np.allclose(np.abs(Rz(np.pi)), np.abs(Z)))

# Bonus: Create |+⟩ from |0⟩ using Ry
ket_0 = np.array([1, 0])
plus = Ry(np.pi/2) @ ket_0
print('\nRy(π/2)|0⟩ =', np.round(plus, 4))
hints: Rz is diagonal with complex exponentials

exercises.title: Bloch Sphere Coordinates
exercises.description: Convert quantum states to Bloch sphere coordinates.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def state_to_bloch(state):
    """
    Convert qubit state to Bloch sphere (x, y, z) coordinates.
    
    For |ψ⟩ = α|0⟩ + β|1⟩:
    x = 2*Re(α*β̄)
    y = 2*Im(α*β̄)
    z = |α|² - |β|²
    """
    state = np.array(state) / np.linalg.norm(state)
    alpha, beta = state[0], state[1]
    
    # TODO: Calculate x, y, z
    x = # Your code
    y = # Your code
    z = # Your code
    
    return x, y, z

# Test with known states
states = {
    '|0⟩': [1, 0],
    '|1⟩': [0, 1],
    '|+⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), 1/np.sqrt(2)],
    '|-⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), -1/np.sqrt(2)],
    '|+i⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), 1j/np.sqrt(2)],
}

for name, state in states.items():
    x, y, z = state_to_bloch(state)
    print(f'{name}: (x={x:.2f}, y={y:.2f}, z={z:.2f})')
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def state_to_bloch(state):
    state = np.array(state) / np.linalg.norm(state)
    alpha, beta = state[0], state[1]
    
    x = 2 * np.real(alpha * np.conj(beta))
    y = 2 * np.imag(alpha * np.conj(beta))
    z = np.abs(alpha)**2 - np.abs(beta)**2
    
    return x, y, z

states = {
    '|0⟩': [1, 0],
    '|1⟩': [0, 1],
    '|+⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), 1/np.sqrt(2)],
    '|-⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), -1/np.sqrt(2)],
    '|+i⟩': [1/np.sqrt(2), 1j/np.sqrt(2)],
}

for name, state in states.items():
    x, y, z = state_to_bloch(state)
    print(f'{name}: (x={x:.2f}, y={y:.2f}, z={z:.2f})')
